title: Effects of Switching Formation
text: This section examines which teams switched formations most frequently, how formation-switching correlated with play type distributions, and uses the 2022 NFL season as a focused case study to illustrate how execution outweighs unpredictability.

title: Effects of Switching Formation
text:
Formation Switching Behavior
Examining efficiency on 3rd and short when repeating formations bring us to the most natural follow-up question, what happens when teams switch their formation? Analyzing plays where teams switched formation relative to their previous third and short attempt versus staying in the same formation reveals patterns that have direct implications for how coaches should think about formation sequencing rather than viewing each formation selection as an isolated decision.



Conversion Rates for each Formation Pattern, 2016–2025





Switching Rates by result for each Formation Play Combination, 2016–2025


Examining formation switching patterns across the 2016-2025 reveals consistent strategic logic in how coaches sequence formations and call plays within each transition pattern. Coaches decided to switch from under center to shotgun about twice as often as switching from shotgun to under center, regardless of the outcome. Breaking this down by the specific prior play type reveals that the switching behavior is remarkably consistent within each formation regardless of whether the previous play was a run or pass. Shotgun Pass and Shotgun Run show nearly identical switching rates after both success and failure with 31.8 and 30.4 percent after failure, and 32.2 and 32.4 percent after success respectively. This suggests that coaches make formation switching decisions based on the formation itself rather than the specific play type that preceded it. The obvious preference for shotgun reflects the evolution of general shotgun usage league-wide.
Across the decade, the run-pass distribution within each formation transition is very similar. Whether teams switch to or stay in shotgun, the majority of plays are passes, 67.0 percent and 64.7 percent respectively, demonstrating shotgun’s inherent association with spread passing concepts regardless of how the formation is reached. We see an inverse relationship when teams switch from shotgun to under center, with 79.8 percent of plays being runs. The same dynamic exists when teams stay under center, running 78.1 percent of the time. These distributions provide evidence confirming the traditional idea that teams predominantly call under center when they want to run the ball and call shotgun when they want to pass.
The conversion rates also validate the intuitive assumption that run plays on 3rd and short are more effective than pass plays. Table 3 shows that runs convert at meaningfully higher rates than passes across every formation transition pattern. Even when making what defenses might consider the most telegraphed play call, switching from shotgun to under center and running the ball, teams still converted at 69.4 percent, the highest conversion rate of any formation pattern and play combination in the dataset. These findings reinforce that short yardage remains a run-favorable situation regardless of formation context. The conversion rates for run plays across all formation patterns are very similar, while pass plays from shotgun are roughly 5% less effective than pass plays out of under center. The two lowest converting formation patterns and play types across the entire decade are passing out of shotgun at 52.4% and 52.8%, suggesting that it is the least effective of the four formation-play combinations in short yardage.

title: Effects of Switching Formation
text:
Why 2022?
When examining the data, the 2022 season provides the richest case study in the dataset for understanding how formation switching interacts with play-calling predictability. Teams that switched to shotgun after using under center on the previous drive experienced a 10.8% dropoff in conversion rate on average, as shown below.
  
2022 serves as a great case study to illustrate the importance of executing the play, rather than trying to achieve a more even distribution of play types. Figure 6 shows the proportion of pass plays called out of Shotgun was a few percentage points below the decade average, suggesting that the passing out of shotgun was slightly less predictable. Despite that, the conversion rates shown in Figure 6 were 3-4% below the decade averages from Table 3. When switching to shotgun from under center, run plays converted at 61.3 percent, 5.2 percentage points below the decade average for that pattern. Conversely, the proportion of run plays called from Under Center was a few percentage points above the decade average, but they were still 3% more effective than the average when switching to Under Center and 9% more effective when staying under center. These trends collectively imply that above everything, establishing an efficient run game is essential for setting up success in key down and distance scenarios like third and short. Without it, defenses can commit to the pass against repeated shotgun concepts without being afraid of getting caught off guard by effectively designed runs from the shotgun.

title: Abstract
text:
Shotgun or Under Center: A Machine Learning Framework for Situational Formation Decision-Making in the NFL

  Chris Antosiewicz
  Syracuse University
  Department of Sport Analytics
  Thesis Advisor: Rodney Paul
  2026

  

  
    This thesis analyzes the strategic decision between shotgun and under center formations in the National Football League using fifteen years of play-by-play data from 2011 through 2025. Through descriptive analysis of historical trends, logistic regression, decision tree modeling, and Random Forest simulation, I construct a formation recommendation system that estimates Expected Points Added and conversion probability for each of four formation-play type combinations – Shotgun Pass, Shotgun Run, Under Center Pass, and Under Center Run – across any down, distance, field position, and game state scenario. The system incorporates a quarterback quality composite metric, stochastic simulation across 10,000 iterations, and a reliability classification framework that communicates uncertainty transparently rather than generating false precision in situations without significant sample size. Wrapped into as an interactive Shiny application, the tool provides coaches and analysts with an evidence-based starting point for formation decisions grounded in historical NFL outcomes.
  

  

  
    Keywords: NFL, formation tendency, Expected Points Added, Random Forest, play-by-play, play-calling, shotgun, under center, stochastic simulation

title: Recommendation App
text: An interactive tool that takes any game situation as input and returns ranked formation-play call recommendations, uncertainty estimates, and reliability warnings — powered by the eight Random Forest models trained in this thesis.

title: Recommendation App
text:
How to Use the App
The app has two main panels: the Game Situation input panel above, and the results panels below. Enter your scenario using the inputs below, then click Get Recommendation.

  📋 Input Reference Guide
  



Input
What to Enter
Example




Down
The current down (1st through 4th)
3rd


Yards to Go
Yards needed for a first down or touchdown
3


Yards from Opponent End Zone
Distance from the opponent's end zone (1 = goal line, 99 = own 1-yard line)
35


Score Differential
Your team's score minus the opponent's score. Negative = trailing.
-7 (trailing by a touchdown)


Seconds Remaining in Half
Seconds left in the current half (0–1800). Two-minute warning = 120.
450 (7:30 left)


Quarter
Current quarter (1–4)
4


QB Quality Tier
Select the tier that best describes your quarterback's skill level
Above Average


Simulations
Number of Monte Carlo simulations (more = slower but more precise). At least 1,000 recommended.
10,000

title: Recommendation App
text:
Understanding the Outputs
Once you click Get Recommendation, the app produces five output panels:

Situation Summary — confirms the game situation you entered as labeled value boxes (down & distance, field zone, score differential, time remaining)
Primary Recommendation — the top-ranked formation-play type combination for this situation, with expected EPA and conversion probability, combined with a backup play and its respective predicted EPA
Data Reliability — flags any formation options with insufficient training data, so you know which recommendations are data-supported and which should be treated with caution
Full Rankings — a complete table ranking all four options (Shotgun Pass, Shotgun Run, Under Center Pass, Under Center Run) by mean EPA, with confidence intervals, conversion probability, boom/bust rates, and reliability tier
EPA Distributions, Mean EPA with CI, Conversion Probability, Training Sample Size — four diagnostic charts showing the underlying uncertainty in each recommendation


QB Quality Tier Reference



Tier
Approx. EPA/play score
Example calibration




Elite
~+1.40
Top-5 QB season


Above Average
~+0.72
Starting caliber, performing well


Average
~+0.13
Median NFL starter


Below Average
~−0.36
Struggling starter or backup


Replacement Level
~−1.20
Practice squad / emergency starter

title: Recommendation App
Modeling Formation Tendency
text: This section builds logistic regression and decision tree models to predict which formation a team will use in a given situation, evaluates how well situational factors explain formation choice, and diagnoses why under center usage is particularly difficult to predict.

title: Modeling Formation Tendency
text:
Overview: Descriptive vs. Predictive Modeling
Having established the historical context for formation evolution and situational tendencies, the analysis now turns to quantifying the situational drivers of formation selection with greater statistical precision. This descriptive modeling phase serves a dual purpose: it validates that the situational variables available in the nflfastR dataset are genuinely predictive of formation choice, and it produces interpretable coefficient estimates that quantify the significance of each factor’s influence on the shotgun versus under center decision.
The modeling dataset was constructed from plays spanning the 2016 through 2025 seasons, filtered to standard run and pass attempts and cleaned of special teams plays, penalties, kneels, and spikes. An 80-20 temporal train-test split was applied, with seasons 2016 through 2023 comprising the training set of 269,903 plays and seasons 2024 through 2025 forming the test set of 68,343 plays. This segmentation was chosen to capture relevant historical trends without overfitting on the high under center usage of the early 2010’s, and provide a large enough sample size for all critical down and distance scenarios to generalize for future seasons with evolving offensive and defensive tendencies. The training set showed 65.8 percent shotgun usage while the test set showed 68.4 percent, a modest distributional shift consistent with the continued growth in shotgun adoption documented in the trend analysis.
Six predictor variables were included across all formation tendency models: down as a categorical variable with four levels, yards to go as a continuous variable, field position measured as yards from the opponent’s end zone, score differential as a signed continuous variable, quarter, and seconds remaining in the half as a continuous variable. These variables collectively capture the game state information most directly relevant to formation decisions without requiring proprietary data unavailable in the public dataset.

title: Modeling Formation Tendency
section: Logistic Regression Model: Quantifying Formation Drivers
text:
Logistic Regression Model: Quantifying Formation Drivers
The first modeling approach applied binary logistic regression predicting the shotgun indicator(0 or 1) from the six situational variables. Logistic regression is well-suited to this initial descriptive purpose because its coefficients are directly interpretable as log-odds ratios that directly describe how each predictor impacts the probability of calling shotgun in terms coaches and analysts can intuitively grasp.



Logistic Regression Coefficient and Odds Table


The model achieved an overall accuracy of 70.5 percent on the test set with an AUC of 0.749, indicating the situational variables collectively explain a substantial portion of the formation selection decision. All coefficient estimates were statistically significant at p < 0.0001 apart from quarter at 0.13, confirming that almost every predictor adds genuine information about formation likelihood beyond what the others capture.
The odds ratios tell the most direct story about formation selection logic. The third down coefficient produces an odds ratio of 14.67, meaning that holding all other variables constant, a play on third down is nearly fifteen times more likely to be run from shotgun than a comparable first down play. This is the largest single effect in the model by a considerable margin and reflects the NFL’s near-universal adoption of shotgun as the default formation on critical conversion downs. Second down shows an odds ratio of 2.74 and fourth down 3.72, both substantial but considerably smaller than the third down effect. Despite fourth down carrying higher stakes, its odds ratio is lower because fourth down situations have a higher proportion of short yardage situations.
The yards to go coefficient produces an odds ratio of 1.18 per yard, meaning each additional yard needed for a first down increases the probability of shotgun being called by 18 percent. This compounds across the realistic range of distances. A team facing ten yards to go is approximately 4.4 times(1.18^9) more likely to align in shotgun than a team facing one yard to go in the same other situational context. The score differential coefficient of -0.035 confirms that trailing teams are slightly more likely to deploy shotgun, with each point of deficit incrementally increasing shotgun probability, a relationship that makes intuitive sense as when teams are down they prioritize getting as much yardage as possible per play.
While the coefficients for field position and time remaining in the half seem meaningless at -.0025 and -.0005 respectively, using the odd ratios to calculate the difference helps quantify what they actually mean. A team at the opponents’ 1 yard line is roughly 11.5%(0.997549^-1) less likely to use shotgun than at the 50 yard line, ceteris paribus. While the effect per yard is negligible, it accumulates across the field such that a team at the opponent’s one-yard line shows meaningfully lower shotgun probability than the same team at midfield. With 1,680 seconds being the difference between the start of a half at 1,800 seconds and the two minute warning at 120 seconds, a team with a full half ahead of them is 43.2%(0.99951680^-1) less likely to use shotgun than a team in the final two minutes, ceteris paribus. This relationship captures what we see on TV, that in the two-minute drill, shotgun usage is much more prominent, reflected in the decision tree analysis below.

title: Modeling Formation Tendency
section: Precision, Recall, and the Under Center Prediction Problem
text:
Precision, Recall, and the Under Center Prediction Problem
While overall accuracy of 70.4 percent appears strong for a six-variable model, disaggregating performance by predicted class reveals an important asymmetry with direct implications for understanding how predictable each formation type has become. The model achieved a recall of 84.7% for shotgun plays. However, the effective recall was far weaker for under center plays, with the model correctly identifying only 39.6% of actual under center plays as captured by the specificity metric.
To look further into this, I wanted to examine when the model was highly confident about its prediction, which I defined as predicted probability below 0.33(confident in under center) or above 0.67(confident in shotgun). Restricting evaluation to the 48.6 percent of predictions where the logistic model expressed high confidence, overall accuracy improved to 85.1 percent with shotgun recall of 97.4%, but the distribution of confident predictions was almost entirely shotgun. Of 33,242 high-confidence predictions, 31,364 were confident shotgun calls and only 1,878 were confident under center calls. Most significantly, the recall for under center plays dropped to 21.6%, reinforcing that the model’s predictive power is asymmetric in a way that reflects the asymmetric usage of each formation type in the modern NFL.



Stacked Bar Chart Showing Model Confidence in Shotgun vs. Actual Formation Used


Notably, the logistic model “confidently” predicted only ~5% of the actual under center plays, and was “unsure” of its prediction for ~75% of actual under center plays compared to only ~40% of shotgun plays. This drastic difference reflects the reality that under center usage has become so situationally selective, depending mainly on the coaches schematic preferences, that it is difficult to anticipate from game state variables alone. When teams deploy under center formations in the modern NFL, they do so in situations that a league-wide model trained on average behavior cannot consistently recognize as under-center situations. While it’s important to note that the performance of the linear model is skewed as the training data has a majority of shotgun play calls(65.8%), it is still a meaningful result.

title: Modeling Formation Tendency
text:
Improving Specificity With Decision Trees
The logistic regression’s core limitation was its specificity of 39.6 percent on under center plays because linear probability models cannot capture the threshold and interaction effects that govern under center usage in specific situations. A decision tree classifier was trained on the same features and training data, offering the advantage of automatic non-linear partitioning that can identify the specific combinations of situational variables where under center becomes the preferred formation choice. A baseline tree was trained with a complex parameter of 0.001 and max depth of 5.
 To optimize the tree, I then manually tuned hyperparameters sequentially across complexity parameter, maximum depth, minimum split size, and minimum bucket size. The complexity parameter was evaluated across values from 0.00001 to 0.005, with accuracy and AUC both the same for each cp value. 0.002 was chosen as the value with maximum accuracy and specificity. Maximum depth testing showed accuracy plateaued after depth 10. Minimum split was set at 9,000 observations, 3.33% of the training data, ensuring that each node split was supported by sufficient observations to represent a genuine population pattern rather than overfitting on a tiny niche of data. Minimum bucket was set at 4,000 observations per leaf node after testing values from 500 to 7,000. For all hyperparameters, when choosing between values I accepted 0.1% drops in overall accuracy for at least one additional percent of specificity.



Performance Comparison Between Logistic Regression and Optimized Tree


The optimized tree achieved an improved accuracy of 71.4% and AUC of 0.757 on the test set. More importantly for this application, specificity improved dramatically from 39.6% to 64.3%. This improvement came at the cost of recall declining from 84.7 to 74.7 percent, reflecting the precision-recall tradeoff inherent in improving under center detection. The log loss from 0.537 to 0.522, confirming that the tree’s probability estimates were better calibrated overall.

title: Modeling Formation Tendency
text:
Decision Tree Model
 The decision tree’s structure itself communicates how teams actually select formations in ways that complement the logistic regression’s coefficient estimates. The root split on down is expected with third down immediately routing 20.5 percent of all plays into a high-shotgun node with 88.9 percent shotgun probability, compared to 59.8 percent for all other downs. The most revealing subsequent splits occur within the first and second down subsets where formation choice is most contested.
Within plays not on third down, the second major split occurs at half seconds remaining below 119.5 seconds – the two-minute warning threshold. Plays within the final two minutes of a half show 89.1 percent shotgun probability, quantifying the near-complete abandonment of under center in clock-management situations. For plays outside the two-minute drill, the tree’s next split involves yards to go exceeding 10.5, routing long-distance plays into an 80.8 percent shotgun node that reflects the pass-oriented response to difficult distances.
The most meaningful split for understanding when teams choose under center occurs deeper in the tree within the balanced score differential, normal time remaining, and manageable distance subpopulation. Here the tree splits on score differential below -7.5, identifying situations where teams are trailing by more than one touchdown increases shotgun probability to 68.3%. For one-possession game scenarios that comprise most offensive plays, plays on first, second, and fourth down show a near-even 50.1 to 49.9 split between shotgun and under center that captures the genuine formation flexibility that exists on standard plays away from the two-minute drill and extreme score situations. The tree identified that under center dominates on first down and fourth down, in short to medium yardage scenarios, and when teams with a big lead try to run down the clock.
The tree ranked variable importance as: Down – 8576.57, Yards to Go – 7330.60, Seconds Remaining in Half – 4377.81, Score Differential – 2179.89, and Yard Line – 458.64.

title: Modeling Formation Tendency
text:
Model Disagreements
The models disagreed on 19.7% of test set plays, 13,455 plays where the logistic regression and optimized tree predicted different formation outcomes. In the situations where disagreement was most frequent, the decision tree was correct more often. 1st and long(7-10 yards), 3rd and short, and 2nd and medium made up 88% of those disagreements, with 1st and long specifically making up 65%. On 1st and long, the tree correctly predicted formation in 51.6% of contested cases against the logistic model’s 48.4 percent. On third and short, the tree’s advantage grew to 59.9 versus 40.1 percent, where the tree’s ability to model the threshold interaction between the short-yardage context and formation preference clearly outperformed the logistic model’s linear approximation.
##Transition to Predictive Modeling The descriptive modeling established two foundational conclusions that directly motivate the predictive structure developed in the following section. First, formation selection is substantially predictable from situational variables, with the optimized decision tree correctly classifying 71.4% of plays. That confirms the six situational features carry genuine information about coaching decisions. Second, and more importantly for the recommendation system, the under center prediction problem revealed that formations are used selectively enough that they retain situational specificity and strategic value.
The logistic model’s difficulty in predicting under center usage is not a failure but a demonstration that under center has become a specialized formation closely tied to coach preferences, where even a well-tuned model cannot consistently anticipate it. If under center usage is inherently less predictable from game state variables, meaning coaches call it in ways that situational data alone cannot fully explain, then performance advantages observed in short yardage, goal line, and formation-switching contexts are likely inflated in general comparisons.
Under-center formations are used selectively in situations where coaches perceive tactical advantages like short-yardage power running with elite offensive line personnel, meaning observed under-center performance reflects both the formation’s effectiveness and the favorability of situations in which coaches choose to employ it. The predictive models must account for this by estimating expected outcomes for each formation-play type combination while acknowledging that historical usage patterns embed strategic information that raw situational variables cannot fully capture.
The following section switches the focus to developing four Random Forest models predicting Expected Points Added and four parallel models predicting yards gained for each formation-play type combination, trained on the 2016 through 2023 seasons and evaluated against a 2024 through 2025 test set. Together these models form a play recommendation system, translating the situational patterns documented in this descriptive analysis into actionable formation-specific outcome predictions with associated uncertainty estimates.

title: Table of Contents
text: A complete guide to the sections of this thesis — click any entry to navigate directly to that page.

title: Table of Contents
section: Shotgun or Under Center: A Machine Learning Framework for Situational Formation Decision-Making in the NFL
text:
Shotgun or Under Center: A Machine Learning Framework for Situational Formation Decision-Making in the NFL
Chris Antosiewicz | Syracuse University | 2026


  1
  
    Abstract
    — A concise summary of the research questions, methodology, key findings, and contributions of this thesis.
  



  2
  
    Introduction
    — Motivates the study of NFL formation usage, introduces the shotgun vs. under center decision, and outlines the analytical framework.
  



  3
  
    Trends in League Usage (2011–2025)
    — Tracks the league-wide shift toward shotgun formations, disaggregates pass and run usage, and identifies the EPA paradox surrounding the growth in shotgun usage.
  



  4
  
    3rd and Short Analysis
    — Examines the conversion rate gap between formations on 3rd and short, explores streak and repetition patterns, and quantifies the value of unpredictability.
  



  5
  
    Formation Switching
    — Analyzes how teams that switch formations across the course of a game differ in play type distributions and conversion rates from those that stay fixed.
  



  6
  
    Goal Line Efficiency
    — Compares touchdown rates by formation and play type in goal line situations, examines variance, and explains the shotgun run paradox near the end zone.
  



  7
  
    Modeling Formation Tendency
    — Builds logistic regression and decision tree models to predict formation choice, evaluates their results, and identifies why under center usage is harder to predict.
  



  8
  
    Predictive Modeling
    — Details the Random Forest architecture, QB quality score construction, the scramble contamination problem, the simulation framework, and the reliability lookup system.
  



  9
  
    Model Validation
    — Contextualizes R² values for EPA prediction, validates model performance across situational cells, analyzes systematic errors, and describes residual distributions.
  



  10
  
    Results & Key Findings
    — A summary of the most important numbers, tables, and conclusions from all sections of the thesis.
  



  11
  
    Limitations
    — A summary of the thesis' limitations.
  



  12
  
    Play Recommender
    — An embedded interactive Shiny application that takes game-situation inputs and returns ranked formation-play call recommendations with uncertainty estimates.

title: Limitations
text: This section addresses the limitations surrounding the data used to complete this  project.

title: Limitations
text:
Selection Bias and Observational Data
The most fundamental limitation of this analysis is that formation choices in NFL games are not randomly assigned. Teams call Shotgun Runs in specific strategic contexts, often audibling when defenses are in pass-prevent alignments, when disguising run intentions from a spread formation provides a tactical advantage, or when the offense believes the defense has over-committed to pass coverage. This means that the 39,915 Shotgun Run plays in the training data are a systematically selected subset of situations, not a representative sample of what would happen if Shotgun Run were called randomly across all situations.
This selection bias affects every formation-play type but with different magnitudes. Under Center Pass plays are called disproportionately in play-action situations against run-committed defenses, inflating their observed EPA relative to what would occur if Under Center Pass were called in neutral situations. The bootstrap analysis of second and long Under Center Pass plays found a mean EPA of +0.127 — a figure that likely reflects the favorable defensive alignments these plays typically face rather than the intrinsic value of the formation choice. A randomized experiment assigning formations without regard to situational context would produce different performance estimates, but such experimental designs are neither feasible nor ethical in NFL game settings.
The appropriate response to this limitation is cautious interpretation rather than flat rejection of the model. The models accurately predict yards and EPA to expect given historical play-calling patterns in each situation. They should be understood as answering the question of what outcomes teams have historically achieved when calling each formation in each situation, not what outcomes would occur if formations were called in structurally different contexts. Coaches using the system should treat recommendations as informed suggestions using historical performance rather than causal estimates of formation effects.

title: Limitations
text:
Missing Variables
The seven variable feature set captures game state adequately but omits several factors that professional NFL analysts and coaching staffs treat as primary inputs to formation decisions. Adjusting to the defensive personnel package, whether the defense has deployed base, nickel, dime, or goal-line personnel, is arguably the single most important observable pre-snap factor in formation selection. Calling Shotgun Pass against dime coverage creates fundamentally different dynamics than calling it against base defense, yet the model treats both identically. Similarly, offensive personnel grouping, pre-snap motion usage, weather conditions for outdoor stadiums, and specific matchup information between offensive skill players and defensive backs are absent from the feature set by virtue of their unavailability in the public nflfastR database.
The omission of player-level quality beyond quarterback also constrains model precision. The QB quality score introduced in this project captures one dimension of personnel advantage, but receiver corps quality, offensive line pass protection ratings, and running back effectiveness against specific defensive fronts all influence formation-play type EPA in ways the models cannot capture. Future work incorporating Next Gen Stats player tracking data or proprietary performance ratings could address these gaps, though at the cost of accessibility to public data that makes this analysis replicable.

title: Limitations
text:
Temporal Validity
The training period spanning 2016 through 2023 captured significant offensive evolution, but models trained on this window may inadequately represent the current state of NFL offense and defense. Defensive adaptation to shotgun formations has accelerated since 2020, with increasingly sophisticated disguised coverages and hybrid defensive personnel packages specifically designed to counter spread concepts. The systematic overestimation of Shotgun Run conversion probability in the 20-40 percent range during test set calibration analysis reflected exactly this temporal lag. Runs from shotgun that converted at certain rates during 2016-2018 became less effective as defenses adapted, and models trained on the full historical window incorporated those earlier rates alongside the declining recent ones.
Implementation of this recommendation system would require periodic model retraining on rolling windows of recent seasons, dropping the earliest years of the training window as new seasons accumulate. An annual retraining cycle updating all eight Random Forest models on the most recent four or five seasons could maintain calibration against the current offensive and defensive environment. This limitation is common to all sports prediction models and does not fundamentally undermine the analytical approach, but it does constrain the shelf life of static models in a constantly evolving league.

title: Limitations
text:
Pistol Formation Classification
A technical limitation inherited from the nflfastR data source affects the precision of the shotgun classification. The binary shotgun indicator in nflfastR aggregates both traditional shotgun formations, where the quarterback aligns five to seven yards behind the center, and pistol formations, where the quarterback aligns three to four yards behind center with a running back immediately behind them. Pistol formations have unique blocking angles, run path options, and play-action mechanics that differentiate them from both traditional shotgun and under center alignments. Lumping pistol plays into the shotgun category introduces classification noise into the Shotgun Run and Shotgun Pass models, potentially obscuring differences between these structurally distinct approaches to quarterback alignment. Teams with heavy pistol usage, like the Baltimore Ravens and San Francisco 49ers, may show systematically different performance patterns within the Shotgun Run category that the current architecture cannot resolve. This limitation could be addressed through supplementary personnel data sources that distinguish pistol from shotgun alignments at the play level, though such data was not available at the scale needed for this analysis.

title: Limitations
text:
Scope of the Recommendation System
The recommendation system as designed optimizes for EPA and conversion probability given the situational inputs provided. It does not account for several strategic considerations that coaching staffs weigh in selecting formations. Establishing running game effectiveness to create play-action opportunities later in the game is a well-documented strategic rationale for calling Under Center Runs with negative expected EPA. Making the decision to sacrifice the value of one play for success later in the game is something made by coaches and while the system cannot model strategic considerations outside of a single-play perspective, it presents coaches with the information to make those decisions themselves.
Similarly, the system treats each play call as independent rather than as part of a drive or game-level sequence. Down and distance situations within a drive are directly correlated – a failed Shotgun Run on first down creates the second and long context that the system then analyzes independently. A more complete model would incorporate drive-level or game-level optimization, recommending formation strategies across sequences of plays rather than optimizing each play in isolation. This extension, while theoretically appealing, would substantially increase model complexity and was beyond the scope of this initial analysis. The current system is best understood as a situational baseline recommendation tool that quantifies historical formation-play type performance and communicates uncertainty transparently, providing coaches and analysts with an evidence-based starting point for formation decisions that they will appropriately adjust based on game-specific context the models cannot observe.

title: Results & Key Findings
text: A summary of the most important numbers, figures, and conclusions from across all sections of this thesis.

title: Results & Key Findings
section: Important Insights from Trends and Descriptive Models
text:
Important Insights from Trends and Descriptive Models
The fifteen-year trend analysis established that the NFL’s shift toward shotgun formations is real, sustained, and structurally significant — but its consequences are more paradoxical than the raw usage numbers suggest. Shotgun’s rise from 40.7% to 72.2% between 2011 and 2023 coincided with no corresponding improvement in league-wide EPA, and 2023 simultaneously represented peak shotgun usage and the worst average EPA year in the dataset, suggesting that widespread adoption has neutralized the formation’s advantages as defenses adapted. The streak and formation-switching analysis suggests that execution outweighs unpredictability — under center converted third and short at nearly identical rates on first and third consecutive usage, and the most telegraphed play in the dataset, switching from shotgun to under center and running, still converted at 69.4%, the highest rate of any formation-switching pattern. At the goal line, shotgun runs surprisingly led all combinations at 54.8% TD rate, likely inflated by quarterback runs and RPO concepts in a compressed field, but under center runs were the most consistent year over year with a standard deviation of just 2.4 percentage points. Shotgun pass was the clear worst combination at the goal line at 45.5%, yet coaches called it at nearly equal rates to shotgun runs despite the ~9% efficiency gap.
The descriptive models quantified what drives formation selection most. Third down makes a play nearly 15 times more likely to come from shotgun, and the two-minute warning cuts under center usage dramatically. The decision tree confirmed these intuitions structurally, identifying down as the single most important variable, followed by yards to go, seconds remaining, and yard line ranked in clear last. Under center usage was concentrated on first, third, and fourth down in short to medium yardage with a manageable score differential, and the near-even 50.1% to 49.9% shotgun split in standard one-possession game situations confirmed that genuine formation flexibility exists away from extreme game states. The tree’s ability to model threshold interactions gave it a 59.9% versus 40.1% advantage in third and short scenarios.

title: Results & Key Findings
text:
Formation-Play Performance Across Situations
The trained predictive models, validated against 2024-2025 test data, produced a systematic picture of formation-play type effectiveness across down and distance situations that aligned with football intuition in expected places while revealing non-obvious findings in others. On first and ten from midfield with an average quarterback, Shotgun Pass generated the highest predicted EPA at approximately +0.09, followed by Under Center Pass at +0.06, Shotgun Run at -0.02, and Under Center Run at -0.05. This ordering of both pass formations outperforming both run formations with shotgun variants outperforming under center variants within each play type, held consistently across most standard first and second down situations.
Short-yardage situations on third and fourth down represented the most consistent deviation from the pass-dominates pattern. On third and short, Under Center Run and Shotgun Run both showed positive conversion probabilities above 55 percent in the simulation, while the conversion probability advantage of passing formations narrowed substantially from its medium and long distance levels. The models’ reliability classifications correctly identified the Shotgun Run training sample on third and medium as insufficient for confident recommendations, preventing the system from generating the incorrect Shotgun Run recommendations that the pre-reliability-adjustment model had produced systematically.
The QB quality score added meaningful differentiation to formation recommendations. Testing the recommendation function with Elite QB quality score inputs produced materially different rankings than Replacement Level inputs in passing-heavy situations, with Shotgun Pass EPA predictions approximately 0.15 to 0.20 EPA units higher for Elite quarterbacks than Replacement Level counterparts in equivalent situations. This differentiation was particularly pronounced in third and medium situations where quarterback decision-making and accuracy under pressure most directly determine conversion outcomes.

title: Results & Key Findings
text:
Risk-Reward Profiles by Formation-Play Type
The 10,000-simulation EPA distributions produced by the stochastic framework revealed meaningful differences in risk-reward profiles across formation-play types that mean EPA comparisons alone would miss. Shotgun Pass distributions showed the widest variance, with 90 percent confidence intervals spanning approximately 3 to 4 EPA units in typical situations. Under Center Run distributions were narrowest, with 90 percent confidence intervals spanning 1.5 to 2 EPA units. This variance structure means that Shotgun Pass offers the highest ceiling and lowest floor, while Under Center Run offers the most consistent predictable outcomes.
The probability of a bust outcome, ranged from 8 to 16 percent for Shotgun Pass depending on the situation, compared to 4 to 6 percent for Under Center Run. The probability of a big play outcome above +1.5 EPA showed the reverse pattern, with Shotgun Pass generating big plays in 14 to 18 percent of simulations against 6 to 9 percent for Under Center Run. These distributional differences, surfaced explicitly in the recommendation output alongside mean EPA and conversion probability, allowed the system to communicate the full strategic picture of each formation choice rather than reducing it to a single ranking metric.

title: Trends in League Usage (2011–2025)
text: This section documents the historical shift in NFL formation usage from 2011 to 2025, separates pass and run trends within each formation, and explores the EPA paradox that emerges when shotgun dominance is examined through an efficiency lens.

title: Trends in League Usage (2011–2025)
section: The Rise of the Shotgun Formation
text:
The Rise of the Shotgun Formation
One of the most visually striking findings from the play-by-play data spanning 2011 through 2025 is the scale and consistency of the NFL’s shift toward shotgun formations. In 2011, 40.7 percent of all offensive plays were run from shotgun — a formation then associated primarily with passing situations and two-minute drills. This evolution was catalyzed by an influx of mobile quarterbacks and the adoption of more college-style spread schemes designed to aid the development of college quarterbacks. This resulted in a near 20% jump from 2011-2013, followed by a more gradual and linear increase before peaking in 2023 at 72.2 percent, representing a 31.4% increase in shotgun usage across just twelve seasons.



Shotgun usage rate across the NFL, 2011–2025


The data in Figure 1 illustrates this evolution across the full fifteen-year window. Shotgun usage notably fell back to 59.4 percent after reaching 64.3 percent the prior season, before resuming its upward trajectory through 2020-2022 where it stabilized in the 66-68 percent range. The 2023 season represented a new peak at 72.2 percent, with a slight pullback in 2024 and 2025. These momentary declines represent the effect of defensive coordinators inevitably adapting their personnel and schemes to neutralize the formation’s advantages.The aggregate trend, however, doesn’t illustrate the meaningful trends for passing and running plays. Disaggregating by play type reveals that the shotgun revolution unfolded along two parallel but distinct tracks.

title: Trends in League Usage (2011–2025)
text:
Pass vs. Run Disaggregation



Shotgun usage rate across the NFL by play type, 2011–2025


For passing plays, shotgun was already the default in 2011 at 58.4 percent, and the trajectory moved slowly but steadily toward complete dominance, reaching 86.2 percent by 2023. The more dramatic story is in the running game. In 2011, only 16.5 percent of run plays originated from shotgun, it was genuinely a formation reserved for obvious passing situations. By 2023, 52.7 percent of designed run plays came from shotgun, crossing the majority threshold for the first time. This shift in run-formation philosophy represents perhaps the most significant tactical evolution in the dataset, as it suggests that the NFL has fundamentally reconsidered whether traditional under center alignments are necessary for effective ground attacks. The 2025 data shows a modest pullback on run plays to 44.1 percent, reflecting a return to more traditional run concepts with heavier personnel as defenses improve their recognition and containment of shotgun run concepts.

title: Trends in League Usage (2011–2025)
text:
The EPA Paradox



Shotgun usage rate vs. league-wide average EPA, 2011–2025


Data from 2023 quantifies the effect of defensive adaptation, as it represents the peak of shotgun usage at 72.2 percent but also the most negative average EPA year in the dataset at -0.035. Shotgun’s advantages may be partially due to the favorable situations in which it is disproportionately used rather than to any intrinsic superiority of the formation itself. As more teams moved to predominantly use shotgun, its advantage has diminished. The ability of shotgun-heavy schemes to create more explosive plays has been neutralized by the widespread adoption of two high shells, among other defensive adjustments. Focusing on which situations each formation-play type generates superior expected outcomes provides the most impact for play-by-play decision making.
